question: Explain the difference between configurable and fixed priority exceptions in ARM Cortex-M.
expectedPoints: Fixed priority: Reset, NMI, HardFault - cannot be changed by software
Configurable priority: All other exceptions and interrupts can be programmed
Fixed priority exceptions use negative priority values (-1 or lower)
Configurable priorities range from 0-15 with 0 being highest

question: What is the purpose of NVIC in ARM Cortex-M processors? List any four features of NVIC.
expectedPoints: Manages all exception and interrupt handling
Supports up to 240 external interrupts (IRQs)
Programmable priority levels for each interrupt
Supports interrupt nesting and tail chaining
Vector table with addresses of exception handlers
Bit-level enable, disable, and pending status registers

question: Differentiate between preemption priority and subpriority with an example.
expectedPoints: Preemption priority: Determines if a higher priority interrupt can interrupt a lower priority handler
Subpriority: Used only when multiple pending interrupts have same group priority
Example: Two interrupts at priority 4 and 5 - if group priority is 4 (preemption), priority 4 can preempt priority 5
When priorities equal (e.g., both at 3), subpriority determines order of servicing
Group priority enables nesting, subpriority resolves tie-breaking

question: Describe the sequence of events when an interrupt occurs in Cortex-M processor.
expectedPoints: Interrupt request (IRQ) sent to NVIC
NVIC checks priority and enables status
If higher priority, NVIC accepts request
Processor completes current instruction
State (registers) pushed onto stack automatically
Processor loads handler address from vector table
Handler executes (ISR)
Handler completes with return to pending state
State popped from stack if no pending exception
Program resumes from interrupted point

question: Explain the late arrival optimization in Cortex-M exception handling.
expectedPoints: Late arrival occurs when higher priority interrupt arrives during state saving of lower priority
Cortex-M automatically detects this situation
Instead of completing lower priority handler first, higher priority is serviced first
Saves time by avoiding redundant state saving/restoring
Improves interrupt response for high priority interrupts
Both handlers eventually execute correctly with optimal performance

question: What is priority grouping? How is it configured in ARM Cortex-M?
expectedPoints: Priority grouping divides priority field into group priority and subpriority
Group priority (preemption level) determines interrupt nesting
Subpriority determines order when multiple pending interrupts with same group priority
Configured via PRIGROUP bits in SCB->AIRCR register
With 4 priority bits: can have 0-7 group priority bits, remainder as subpriority
Allows flexible control of preemption behavior and tie-breaking

question: Write a short note on vector table and its relocation.
expectedPoints: Vector table contains addresses of all exception handlers
Located at fixed memory address (0x00000000 for flash)
First entry: initial Stack Pointer value, then handler addresses
Reset handler at offset 0x04
Can be relocated to SRAM using VTOR register
Useful for bootloaders and dynamic firmware updates
Relocation allows changing interrupt behavior at runtime

question: Explain in detail the various priority masking registers in ARM Cortex-M: PRIMASK, BASEPRI, and FAULTMASK. Include their purpose, usage, and differences.
expectedPoints: PRIMASK: Single bit register, when set masks all configurable priority exceptions (1=mask, 0=enable). Used for critical sections. Only NMI and HardFault can interrupt. Accessible via CPSIE/CPSID instructions or registers.
BASEPRI: Defines minimum priority threshold. When set to value N, all exceptions with priority >= N are masked. Allows selective blocking of lower priority interrupts. Set to 0 to disable masking.
FAULTMASK: Similar to PRIMASK but also masks HardFault (only NMI remains). Used in fault handling routines for critical sections. Automatically cleared on exception return.
Differences: PRIMASK masks all configurable, BASEPRI is configurable level-based masking, FAULTMASK is most restrictive (masks faults too).
Priority values: Lower number = higher priority. Masking works with numerically higher (lower priority) values.

question: Describe the tail chaining mechanism in Cortex-M exception handling. How does it improve performance?
expectedPoints: Tail chaining is an optimization when transitioning between exception handlers.
Scenario: One exception handler completes while another pending exception is waiting.
Without tail chaining: Pop registers → Push registers → Execute new handler
With tail chaining: Skip register pop/push, go directly from old handler to new handler
Benefits: Reduces latency, saves 6-12 clock cycles per transition
Stack frame is reused - only updated if priorities differ
Automatic hardware optimization - no software intervention needed
Also applies when returning to Thread mode with pending interrupts
Improves real-time response in high-frequency interrupt scenarios
Works only when new exception has equal or lower priority than completed one

question: Explain the different types of faults in ARM Cortex-M and how exception priority helps in fault handling.
expectedPoints: Fault Types: 1) HardFault - all types of faults, 2) MemManage - memory protection violations, 3) BusFault - bus errors (memory access), 4) UsageFault - programming errors (undefined instructions, divide by zero)
Fixed Priority: HardFault has priority -1, MemManage -2, BusFault -3, UsageFault -4. All higher than configurable priorities (0-15).
Nesting: With configurable priorities, higher priority faults can preempt lower ones. MemManage can preempt UsageFault if configured with higher priority.
Fault Handling: Use FAULTMASK in handlers to prevent other faults from interrupting
Vector table includes fault handlers at specific offsets
SCB registers provide fault status, address, and configuration
Priority enables: Can enable MemManage, BusFault to occur before HardFault via SCB->SHCSR registers

question: Design an embedded system scenario where priority inversion can occur and explain how to resolve it using priority inheritance or ceiling protocol.
expectedPoints: Scenario: Task A (Low Priority) locks mutex → Task B (Medium Priority) preempts A → Task C (High Priority) tries to lock same mutex → Blocks waiting for Task A → Priority Inversion: Medium task runs while high priority waits for low
Example: Mars Pathfinder mission had this issue causing resets
Solution 1 - Priority Inheritance: When high priority task blocked, low priority task temporarily inherits high priority until mutex released
Solution 2 - Priority Ceiling: Mutex assigned highest priority of any task that uses it; task gains this priority when acquiring mutex
Implementation in Cortex-M: Use OS features like FreeRTOS configUSE_MUTEXES and priority inheritance
With BASEPRI: Temporarily raise low priority task's priority using software before critical section
With semaphore: Use priority inheritance protocol available in RTOS
